refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning sensor acquisition, network transmission, and command execution.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency (sensor → remote operator → command execution) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) matching agreed operational profiles. Validate p95 percentile over minimum 1000 samples per condition. Correlate with operator safety assessments and incident data.
sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire and encode sensor telemetry within latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit telemetry and commands over network link within latency budget
FR-TEL-003: Decode and execute remote commands within latency budget
FR-TEL-004: Monitor and report latency performance to operator

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency sensor-to-command pipeline with hardware-accelerated encoding, priority-queued network transmission, and deterministic command execution scheduling
DP-B: Deploy edge computing node co-located with vehicle to reduce round-trip distance and implement local control fallback with operator override
DP-C: Use adaptive bitrate streaming with predictive compression and network-aware buffering to optimize latency under variable conditions
DP-D: Implement redundant network paths (cellular + satellite) with automatic failover to maintain latency SLA
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement low-latency sensor-to-command pipeline with hardware-accelerated encoding, priority-queued network transmission, and deterministic command execution scheduling

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor acquisition rate and encoding latency (mitigated by hardware acceleration)
Coupling between network jitter and command execution timing (mitigated by deterministic scheduling)
Coupling between telemetry bandwidth and latency (mitigated by priority queuing)
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits loose coupling through separation of concerns: sensor pipeline, network layer, and execution layer are independently optimizable. Hardware acceleration decouples encoding from CPU scheduling. Priority queuing decouples telemetry volume from command latency. Deterministic scheduling decouples network variability from execution timing.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA) is available on vehicle platform; Risk: Legacy equipment may lack acceleration capability
Assumption: Network infrastructure supports priority queuing (QoS); Risk: Public networks may not honor QoS markings
Assumption: Deterministic execution environment achievable; Risk: Real-time OS constraints may conflict with other vehicle functions
Assumption: p95 ≤ 150 ms is achievable with single network path; Risk: Network conditions may exceed latency budget, requiring DP-B or DP-D fallback
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-A should be augmented with explicit fallback strategy: if p95 latency exceeds 150 ms under agreed conditions, trigger DP-B (edge computing) or DP-D (redundant paths) as contingency. Add latency monitoring and adaptive mode switching to DP-A specification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic: sensor encoding, network transmission optimization, command decoding, latency monitoring, and operator feedback. Owns the end-to-end latency budget and interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor/command access) and Network Link (for transmission).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Encoded sensor data (video, position, diagnostics) transmitted from Teleoperation to remote operator via Network Link. Latency-critical; subject to priority queuing and adaptive compression.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Remote operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) received by Teleoperation from operator via Network Link. Decoded and forwarded to Vehicle for execution.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network transport (cellular, satellite, or hybrid). Teleoperation manages QoS, priority marking, and latency monitoring at this interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Latency degradation alerts and teleoperation mode status (nominal, degraded, fallback) transmitted to Safety Services for operator awareness and safety decision-making.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of latency-critical teleoperation logic. All sensor encoding, network optimization, and command decoding are contained. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary for safety coordination.

Vehicle owns sensor hardware and command execution, but latency optimization (encoding, queuing, scheduling) belongs in Teleoperation. Assigning DP-A to Vehicle would scatter latency logic across vehicle firmware and create tight coupling with network layer.

Safety Services consumes latency status via SafetyAlert but does not own latency optimization. Assigning DP-A to Safety Services would create inappropriate responsibility for network and vehicle control logic.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but too coarse-grained for DP-A implementation. Latency optimization requires dedicated module with clear ownership and interface contracts.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module contains all latency-critical logic (sensor encoding, network optimization, command decoding, latency monitoring) and owns the p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA. Vehicle module provides sensor/command interfaces; Network Link provides transport; Safety Services consumes latency status for safety decisions.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a performance bottleneck if encoding/decoding logic is not properly parallelized or hardware-accelerated. Mitigation: Explicit performance testing and profiling during detailed design.

Risk: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient QoS visibility or control for Teleoperation to enforce priority queuing. Mitigation: Define Network Link interface contract to include QoS metadata and latency telemetry.

Risk: Vehicle module may not expose sensor data with sufficient timing precision for Teleoperation to measure and optimize latency. Mitigation: Define Telemetry interface to include timestamp metadata from sensor acquisition.

Risk: Fallback to DP-B (edge computing) or DP-D (redundant paths) may require architectural changes if not anticipated in Teleoperation module design. Mitigation: Design Teleoperation with pluggable network strategy (single path, edge, redundant) to enable future fallback.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Teleoperation module specification should explicitly define: (1) latency budget allocation across sensor encoding, network transmission, and command execution; (2) interface contracts for Telemetry (timestamp precision), Tasking (command priority), and Network Link (QoS metadata); (3) performance requirements for encoding/decoding (throughput, CPU/memory footprint); (4) fallback strategy and mode transitions if p95 latency exceeds 150 ms.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (low-latency pipeline with hardware acceleration, priority queuing, deterministic scheduling) directly addresses FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. DP-A is verifiable through latency measurement and profiling. Coupling analysis confirms loose coupling through separation of concerns. Assumptions and risks are identified and manageable.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If Teleoperation module lacks authority over Network Link QoS, priority queuing logic may be ineffective. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface contract includes QoS control primitives.

Diffusion risk: If Vehicle module does not timestamp sensor data, Teleoperation cannot measure end-to-end latency accurately. Mitigation: Require Telemetry interface to include acquisition timestamps.

Diffusion risk: If Safety Services does not act on SafetyAlert in timely manner, latency degradation may not trigger appropriate safety response. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface with priority levels and expected response times.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-A. External dependencies (Vehicle sensors, Network Link, Safety Services) are minimal and well-defined via interface contracts. No significant architectural diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-008 is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with mock Vehicle and Network Link) and integrated with real Vehicle and network infrastructure. p95 percentile metric is measurable and repeatable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of latency budget to Teleoperation module is clear: module owns sensor encoding, network optimization, and command decoding latencies. Vehicle module owns sensor acquisition and command execution latencies. Network Link owns transmission latency. Safety Services owns latency monitoring and response. Clear ownership boundaries enable independent verification and optimization.

recommended_revisions: Refine Teleoperation module specification to include explicit latency budget allocation (e.g., encoding ≤ 30 ms, transmission ≤ 80 ms, decoding ≤ 30 ms, monitoring ≤ 10 ms).
Define Network Link interface contract to include QoS metadata (priority level, latency telemetry, packet loss rate) required by Teleoperation for optimization.
Define Telemetry interface contract to include sensor acquisition timestamps (with precision ≥ 1 ms) required by Teleoperation for latency measurement.
Specify fallback strategy in Teleoperation module: if p95 latency exceeds 150 ms, trigger edge computing (DP-B) or redundant paths (DP-D) as contingency.
Add latency monitoring and alerting logic to Teleoperation module: continuous measurement of p95 latency, SafetyAlert generation if threshold exceeded, operator notification.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs addressing sensor encoding, network transmission, command decoding, and latency monitoring. DP-A is sound and well-mapped to Teleoperation module, but detailed design requires refinement of latency budget allocation, interface contracts, and fallback strategy. Proceeding to next decomposition level will clarify implementation details and reduce architectural risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire and encode sensor telemetry (video, position, diagnostics) within latency budget (target: ≤ 30 ms) using hardware acceleration where available
FR-TEL-002: Transmit telemetry and commands over network link with priority queuing and QoS optimization within latency budget (target: ≤ 80 ms) under agreed network conditions
FR-TEL-003: Decode remote commands and execute on vehicle within latency budget (target: ≤ 30 ms) with deterministic scheduling
FR-TEL-004: Monitor end-to-end latency performance (p95 percentile) and alert Safety Services if threshold exceeded (target: ≤ 10 ms monitoring overhead)

revise.why: DP-A should be augmented with explicit fallback strategy and latency budget allocation. Current DP-A assumes single network path and deterministic execution environment, which may not be achievable under all agreed network conditions. Recommend adding contingency DPs (edge computing, redundant paths) and adaptive mode switching logic to DP-A specification.

assumptions: Hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA) is available on vehicle platform for sensor encoding
Network infrastructure supports QoS and priority queuing
Deterministic execution environment (real-time OS) is achievable on vehicle
p95 ≤ 150 ms is achievable with single network path under agreed conditions
Operator can safely control equipment with 150 ms latency (validated by safety analysis)
Sensor data can be timestamped with ≥ 1 ms precision for latency measurement
open_risks: Network conditions may exceed latency budget, requiring fallback to edge computing or redundant paths
Hardware acceleration may not be available on legacy equipment, limiting encoding performance
Real-time OS constraints may conflict with other vehicle functions (diagnostics, safety monitoring)
QoS markings may not be honored on public networks, reducing priority queuing effectiveness
Latency monitoring overhead may consume significant CPU/network resources
Safety Services may not respond to latency alerts in timely manner, creating safety gap
evidence_needed: Network latency profile under agreed conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, geographic distance)
Sensor encoding performance benchmarks (latency, throughput, CPU/memory footprint) with and without hardware acceleration
Vehicle command execution timing (sensor acquisition, processing, actuator response) under nominal and degraded conditions
Operator safety assessment: minimum latency tolerance for safe remote operation (human factors study)
Network QoS capability assessment: availability of priority queuing, latency telemetry, failover mechanisms
Real-time OS feasibility study: deterministic scheduling constraints, context switch overhead, integration with other vehicle functions
